title: Why values matter for wellbeing
citation: Hayes, S.C., Strosahl, K.D., & Wilson, K.G. (1999). Acceptance and commitment therapy: An experiential approach to behavior change. Guilford Press.
content:
Values, in the psychological sense, are not opinions or preferences but chosen directions of how a person wants to move through their life. Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, developed by Steven Hayes and colleagues, places values at the centre of its approach to wellbeing. The framework distinguishes values from goals: a goal is a specific outcome that can be achieved and then completed, while a value is an ongoing direction that can never be fully arrived at, only lived toward. Compassion, creativity, courage, and connection are values in this sense. They are not destinations but qualities that can be brought to any action, in any moment.

The psychological significance of values lies in what happens when people act in alignment with them versus when they do not. When a person's daily actions consistently conflict with what they care about most, they often experience a sense of emptiness, meaninglessness, or low-grade dissatisfaction that is difficult to attribute to any single cause. This is not simply a mood state; it is often a signal that the way a person is spending their time and energy has drifted from what they hold as genuinely important.

Acting from values also supports what Hayes calls psychological flexibility, the capacity to remain in contact with the present moment and move toward what matters even in the presence of difficult thoughts, feelings, and circumstances. This is directly relevant to wellbeing because life inevitably involves pain, loss, and situations that cannot be controlled or resolved. Values provide a basis for meaningful action even when circumstances are constrained, because a person can express a value in how they respond to any situation, regardless of outcome.

Research within the ACT framework has found that values clarification and values-consistent action are associated with greater life satisfaction, higher tolerance of difficult internal experiences, and improved functioning across a range of clinical and non-clinical populations.
